bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes coercion and civil‑rights risk, foregrounding union warnings and charged phrases like "untrained, armed" and "lawless threat," portraying the move as discriminatory and dangerous; right-leaning coverage frames it as decisive law‑and‑order action, praising ICE with phrases such as "brilliant and patriotic," stressing enforcement and blaming "radical left Democrats.
Centrists de-emphasize moralizing language, instead noting the funding impasse and officials' clarification that ICE would "assist" with limited duties.

The fault lines—training/operational authority, potential arrests, and racial‑profiling risks—reveal deeper ideological and cultural divides, though all report staffing shortages and continued uncertainty.
summary: On Saturday, March 21, 2026, President Donald Trump threatened to deploy immigration enforcement agents to U.S. airports, posting 'I will move our brilliant and 'patriotic ICE Agents to the Airports where they will do Security like no one has ever seen before'.
Facing a partial DHS shutdown, the 50,000-strong TSA workforce has worked without pay since a funding bill expired five weeks ago and faces missing a second paycheck on March 27.

At major hubs, passengers faced 120-minute waits at George Bush Intercontinental Airport and 80 minutes at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport amid staffing gaps, with over half absent Sunday in Houston and 38% missing Wednesday in Atlanta.

Officials cautioned that some smaller airports could close and wait times won't improve until funding is restored and TSA officers are paid, while Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer proposed funding only TSA as Democrats press for policy changes for ICE.

Negotiators intensified talks when White House border czar Tom Homan met bipartisan senators, Senate Majority Leader John Thune said 'This is a pox on everybody's house', and Elon Musk offered to pay TSA officers' salaries.
title: Source Icon

summary: On Saturday at 7:44 p.m. ET, Trump posted a 48-hour ultimatum on Truth Social demanding Iran reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face strikes on Iranian energy facilities.
Amid soaring energy prices, the Strait of Hormuz handles about one-fifth of global oil and gas shipments, prompting fears of supply disruption and market volatility.

Iran warned that any strike on its energy facilities would prompt attacks on U.S. and Israeli energy and infrastructure assets, while U.S. Central Command said Iran's threat to navigation has been degraded.
Markets show immediate strain as tanker movements halted and the U.S. national gas average reached $3.93, with analysts warning of a 48-hour 'ticking time bomb'.

The administration's rhetoric pivoted to, with Iran's Revolutionary Guards warning the Strait of Hormuz would stay closed until power plants are rebuilt, risking extended disruption, as Trump warns Iran.
title: Trump threatens to 'obliterate' Iran power plants if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened within 48 hours

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes moral outrage—foregrounding Trump's "glad he's dead" post as "jaw‑droppingly vile" and portraying Mueller as a "hero" and integrity‑driven public servant—while right-leaning outlets frame the Russia probe as a "witch hunt," cast Trump’s reaction as understandable and amplify his words.
Rhetorically the left uses condemnatory moral language; the right relies on delegitimizing labels and normalization, with centers reporting more neutrally, calling Mueller "no‑nonsense.
Parkinson’s as a cause is de-emphasized by the left and noted by center/right; all agree Mueller died at 81, revealing deeper ideological and cultural schisms.
summary: On Saturday, March 21, 2026, Trump posted 'He can no longer hurt innocent people!' on Truth Social, signing as President DONALD J. TRUMP, after Mueller's death was announced.
The president's animus traces to Mueller's 2017 appointment as special counsel investigating alleged Russian interference, which has fueled Trump's long-held grudge.
Internet users responded with outrage and a viral meme comparing young Mueller in combat gear with young Trump holding a bowling trophy went viral, while actor Don Cheadle also reacted.
The timing amplified calls for equal treatment from critics demanding equal condemnation after Trump’s Pearl Harbor remark two days earlier, with some invoking the Charlie Kirk comparison.

Mueller had been summoned to testify in a Jeffrey Epstein-related congressional probe but his health prevented it, while his 2019 report and July 24, 2019 House Judiciary Committee appearance provide broader context.
title: Robert Mueller, 9/11-era FBI chief who later probed alleged Trump-Russia ties, dead at 81

bias: Coverage splits sharply: left outlets frame the launches as diplomatic and human‑impact stories—highlighting "dramatic escalation" and labeling the strikes "reckless"—while right outlets cast them as political vindication, using phrases like "proven right" or asserting Tehran "proves it was lying" and amplifying threat with "critical/strategic" language; center reporting is more technical, treating a possible "first operational use of IRBMs" and range implications analytically.

All sides rely on a prominent report and unnamed U.S. officials and note two missiles at ~3,800–4,000 km, but independent sensor corroboration is de‑emphasized and Iranian accounts conflict—differences thus reflect geopolitical and partisan priorities.
summary: On Saturday, Iran fired two intermediate-range ballistic missiles toward the Diego Garcia joint US-UK military base, and said its Natanz nuclear facility was hit in an airstrike with no radiation leakage as the International Atomic Energy Agency urged restraint.
The British government has said it will provide only defensive support while approving U.S. use of two bases, and the U.S. military has struck more than 8,000 targets, Admiral Brad Cooper said.

Analysts said the strike reached about 4,000 km, roughly double the previously assumed 2,000 km range, and The Wall Street Journal reported possible modified or reduced-payload intermediate-range ballistic missiles.
The International Atomic Energy Agency said no abnormal radiation was detected, and G7 foreign ministers pledged support for energy and maritime security.

With oil prices climbing and fuel shortages feared, Israel's defence minister warned of a surge in attacks, while US Central Command reported more than 8000 targets struck, highlighting escalation risks.
title: Iran Fires Ballistic Missiles at Diego Garcia Base

bias: Left-Leaning pieces emphasize hypocrisy and political peril, using charged phrases like "desperate push," "firestorm," and "soaring" to portray the 30‑day waiver as a reactive, morally fraught concession tied to the war; right-leaning coverage frames the same move with pragmatic, consumer-focused language—"keep the price down," "stranded at sea," "skyrocketing energy prices"—portraying it as decisive supply relief.

Pivotal divides center on whether easing sanctions on an adversary is hypocritical or necessary, and on war/Strait‑of‑Hormuz links; centrist accounts de-emphasize partisan heat, focusing on timeframe and market detail.
Both agree it’s a temporary, 30‑day step to free roughly 140 million barrels to ease prices.
summary: Late Friday, the Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control issued a General License permitting purchase and offloading of Iranian and Russian oil already loaded on any vessel until April 19.
Amid a meteoric rise in oil prices, the administration is temporarily lifting bans for next month to curb surges tied to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran and Brent crude at $119 per barrel.

By waiving ten separate sets of sanctions, the move narrowly tailors a short-term authorization applying only to Iranian petroleum already in transit, using stranded barrels to keep prices down while long-standing sanctions remain.

President Donald Trump suggested the relaxation could inject approximately 140 million barrels into global markets while Treasury officials said Tehran won't easily benefit because longstanding banking sanctions cut Iran off from the global financial system.

Framed against broader security needs, officials said the administration is weighing deployment of a second Marine Expeditionary Unit while the Pentagon seeks $200 billion, warning the Strait of Hormuz must be guarded by NATO and allied naval forces.
title: Trump administration temporarily lifts sanctions on Iranian oil at sea amid soaring prices

bias: Coverage split along a human‑impact vs. systemic‑risk axis: left outlets foreground emotive, victim‑centered language—terms like "crashes into" and "wrecked"—and amplified unverified casualty claims , often citing a reported 76–80 aboard ; these form pivotal dividing lines; right outlets emphasize disruption and institutional failure, using forceful verbs such as "hits" or "slams into," highlighting technical details and blaming weather/visibility or systemic safety lapses; center reporting de‑emphasized causal claims and leaned on FAA ground‑stop notices, FlightRadar24 data and ATC audio; all agree a collision caused nose damage, injuries and an FAA ground stop.
summary: A pilot and co-pilot were killed and a sergeant and an officer were injured when an Air Canada passenger plane struck a vehicle on the runway at LaGuardia Airport late Sunday night, according to initial reports.

Authorities including the New York Police Department and New York Fire Department confirmed the incident but released limited details, while a source said 76 passengers and four crew members were on board.
Images from the scene showed damage to the aircraft’s front underside, and the National Transportation Safety Board has begun gathering information on the collision.
title: At least four injured after Air Canada plane collides with vehicle at New York's La Guardia Airport

bias: Left coverage foregrounds moral outrage, labeling the post "insensitive" and "shocking" and saying it "reached a new level," portraying the president as crossing an ethical line; right coverage emphasizes context and justification, highlighting "glad" and claims Mueller "hurt innocent people," using terms like "blasts," "teeth‑baring," or "celebrated" and de‑emphasizing moral censure; center calls the remark "crass" while focusing on biographical context and bipartisan backlash.
Pivotal divides include whether the comment requires moral condemnation versus political appraisal and disputes over Fox News' omission; all sides agree on Mueller's death, the post, and criticism.
summary: Shortly after Robert S. Mueller III's death was announced, Trump posted on Truth Social, 'Good, I'm glad he's dead' and 'He can no longer hurt innocent people!'.
After a long-standing rivalry rooted in the Russia probe, Mueller led the special counsel investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election and its 2019 report, ending in 2019.
Lawmakers across the aisle denounced Trump, with Rep. Dan Goldman and Sen. Adam Schiff criticizing his celebration of Mueller's death, as Brit Hume and Piers Morgan condemned his remarks.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent defended Trump on NBC's Meet the Press, urging empathy for him and his family, while Fox News largely omitted Trump's celebratory post from its on-air coverage.
Mueller's decades-long public service includes directing the FBI from 2001 to 2013 after 9/11, while the Mueller family disclosed his Parkinson's diagnosis in 2021 and 2025 as tributes continued.
title: Trump On Robert Mueller's Death: 'Good, I'm Glad He's Dead.'

bias: Left coverage largely sticks to Tehran's claim, using neutral, attributive wording such as "heavy damage" and de-emphasized quantification, while right coverage amplifies destruction narratives—using "destroyed/destroying," naming officials and citing a quantified 81,365 figure—to stress widespread civilian harm.
Centrists occupy a middle ground, foregrounding escalation language like "threatens to retaliate" and risks to Gulf desalination plants and thousands of targeted sites.

Pivotal divides include reporting of that large figure, attribution versus causal verbs, and retaliation threats; both sides nonetheless agree on the core claim of infrastructure harm and a notable lack of independent verification.
summary: Iran's Energy Minister Abbas Aliabadi stated that US and Israeli strikes caused heavy damage to the country's water and energy infrastructure , targeting dozens of water transmission and treatment facilities and parts of critical water supply networks, with repair efforts underway.
Iran's Red Crescent chief Pir Hossein Kolivand reported that over 81,000 civilian sites, including homes, schools, medical centers, and commercial properties, have been damaged due to these strikes.
title: Iran minister says US, Israeli strikes caused ‘heavy damage’ to water, energy infrastructure

bias: Left-Leaning outlets foreground the family's emotional appeal—calling it "desperate/desperately" and highlighting reported leads framed as possible "proof of life" and suggesting quiet FBI movement; right-leaning coverage emphasizes a stalled probe , law‑and‑order urgency and a retired detective's multi‑accomplice theory with focus on "porch guy" footage, using language like "heartbreaking" and "brought to a final place of rest"; center coverage notes the 84‑year‑old's missing phone and meds and urges "no detail too small.
Both sides amplify calls for "renewed attention," exposing an empathy‑versus‑security divide.
summary: Authorities are searching for 84-year-old Nancy Guthrie , who went missing from her Tucson, Arizona home on February 1st, with footage showing a masked figure outside her home.
Guthrie's daughter Savannah and her siblings have made public appeals for information, offering a $1 million reward in addition to the FBI's $100,000 reward for her return.
Police are asking neighbors for any relevant surveillance footage, notes, or observations that could help find Guthrie, as her family continues to ache and wonder about her whereabouts.
title: Family appeals to Arizona community for clues in ongoing disappearance of Nancy Guthrie

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the sheriff’s removal of ballots as a politically motivated, norm‑threatening act—using terms like 'seizes,' 'unprecedented,' and 'sow distrust,' highlighting his gubernatorial bid and de‑emphasizing legal‑authority details—while right-leaning coverage presents it as an election‑integrity 'crackdown' or bold act 'in defiance,' amplifying scale with figures like '650,000' and citing an alleged '45,000' excess‑votes finding.
Rhetorically, the left moralizes and warns of institutional harm; the right uses law‑and‑order, quantifying language; center frames a contested dispute and flags official criticism.
Both agree ballots were removed, the actor is a GOP candidate, and county officials disputed his claims.
summary: Chad Bianco seized over 500,000 ballots from a special election, citing an investigation into alleged counting discrepancies.
County officials dispute the claims, while Rob Bonta called the move unprecedented and warned it could undermine trust in elections.
The investigation was launched after a complaint about ballot counts in a November 2025 redistricting election.
title: California sheriff running for governor seizes more than a half million ballots from 2025 election

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds personal loss and Pierce’s decade-long warmth in the hockey community, using terms like "beloved" and "devastating" to center grief and legacy, while right-leaning pieces amplify shock and spectacle—deploying vivid metaphors such as "like a tornado of smoke" and highlighting her media profile and podcasting to heighten emotional resonance.

A pivotal divide is investigatory specificity: center and right provide precise scene details and the center names the Minnesota State Fire Marshal's Office, while the right often omits it and the left de-emphasizes those specifics.
Both sides align on the fatalities, emergency response, and NHL condolences.
summary: Firefighters responded to a house fire in White Bear Lake, Minnesota, where they found an adult, three children, and a dog deceased.
NHL reporter Jessi Pierce and her three children were killed in the fatal house fire, according to the NHL.
The Minnesota Wild and NHL colleagues mourned the loss of Pierce, praising her passion for hockey and compassionate personality.
title: Minnesota Wild reporter Jessi Pierce and her 3 children found dead in house fire, league says
Democrats say GOP cuts to Planned Parenthood are harming women’s healthcare and raising costs

summary: On March 21, the UAE Ministry of Defence said its air defences have intercepted 341 ballistic missiles, 15 cruise missiles and 1,748 UAVs since the conflict began, according to an Abu Dhabi statement.
The escalation began on February 28 when strikes killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, prompting Iranian retaliation and the appointment of Mojtaba Khamenei as new Supreme Leader.

Casualties include two members of the UAE armed forces killed, six civilian fatalities involving foreign nationals from Pakistan, Nepal, Bangladesh and Palestine, and 160 injured including nationals from the United Arab Emirates , India, Egypt, the Philippines and Sri Lanka.
On March 21, UAE air defences engaged three ballistic missiles and eight UAVs launched from Iran, and the UAE Ministry of Defence said it remains fully prepared to confront threats.
With regional waterways affected, the escalation has disrupted international energy markets and regional economic stability, as Iran's actions have effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz.
title: UAE Air Defences Intercepted 341 Ballistic Missiles, over 1,700 UAVs Since Start of West Asia Conflict

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the policy shift as a "rollback" of essential "environmental rules," portraying the protest as a virtuous way to "mark World Water Day" against "elite profits.
Conversely, right-leaning narratives de-emphasize ecological impact, using the neutral "withdrawal" of "rules" to support deregulation while characterizing activists as "disruptive opponents.
A pivotal dividing line is the political context; center-leaning reports uniquely highlight that the 43 rescinded items were "midnight decrees" from the previous administration.
All sides emphasize the leader’s "arch-conservative" ideology, reflecting a broader geopolitical clash between deregulation and conservation.
summary: On Sunday, March 22, 2026, thousands of protesters marched in Santiago, Chile, and other cities to mark World Water Day and protest President José Antonio Kast’s withdrawal of environmental decrees.
Conservative President José Antonio Kast withdrew 43 environmental protection regulations signed during Gabriel Boric's government earlier this month.
Environmental rules covered protections for species like Darwin's frog and the Humboldt penguin, created national parks, and outlined decontamination plans for Lake Villarrica.
Organizers warned Chile still has 1.4 million people without drinking water, calling the move an 'environmental chainsaw approach.'
Kast has said he acknowledges climate change but prioritizes economic deregulation and technical criteria over 'environmental ideologies', marking Chile's sharpest rightward turn since 1990.
title: Chileans Mark World Water Day by Protesting President Kast’s Rollback of Environmental Rules

bias: Left outlets frame the Treasury claim as defensive—calling it "Trump's war," using "denies" and the quoted "plenty" to signal skepticism about domestic costs—while right coverage presents "plenty" as reassurance, foregrounding readiness, phrases like "leaving all options on the table," congressional "stiff opposition," and a $200 billion request as support for a strong posture.
Center pieces de-emphasize ownership, quote Bessent calling tax hikes "ridiculous," and stick to attribution.

Pivotal divides—conflict ownership, supplemental dollar size, and tax rhetoric—mirror deeper ideological and geopolitical splits, though all report the funding claim, supplemental ask, and rejection of tax increases.
summary: On Sunday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told NBC News' Meet the Press the U.S. has 'We have plenty of money to fund this war' and ruled out tax increases, saying President Donald Trump is 'leaving all options on the table'.

Pentagon officials are preparing a formal supplemental funding request linked to Operation Epic Fury, with U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth confirming a roughly $200 billion ask facing congressional resistance.
The interview also centered on the administration's oil policy as Bessent defended lifting sanctions on Iran and Russia, citing Treasury analysis that Russia's extra oil revenue would be $2 billion.
U.S. administration officials say early war spending exceeded $11 billion, and Welker warned of potential higher consumer prices as oil could stay above $100, quoting Scott Kirby.

Recent laws and budgets have already expanded U.S. defence spending, including last month’s Fiscal 2026 Defense Appropriations Act with about $840 billion and last summer’s $156 billion defense tax-cut bill, amid a roughly $1 trillion federal military budget this year.
title: Treasury's Bessent says US has 'plenty' of funds for Iran war
U.S. Army Orders 2,500 Skydio Drones in Record $52M Deal

bias: Right-Leaning coverage frames the vote as an embattled triumph—using terms like "survived" and "key test vote," linking advancement to a DHS " shutdown " and labeling Mullin "Trump’s homeland security nominee" to heighten crisis and partisan loyalty; in contrast, center reporting emphasizes institutional process with neutral phrasing like "advances," supplies vote details and notes he "would succeed" the prior secretary, while left-leaning treatment is largely de-emphasized here.
The pivotal divide is whether advancement is routine or existential, yet all agree the nomination moved forward and a final vote is expected.
summary: On Sunday, the U.S. Senate advanced Sen. Markwayne Mullin 's nomination after the president announced the Oklahoma Republican would replace embattled DHS Secretary Kristi Noem.

A bipartisan group of senators has met twice with border czar Tom Homan in recent days as the two sides seek a resolution, but Mullin's confirmation path grew messy last week amid disputes and negotiations.

In a 54 to 37 vote, the U.S. Senate advanced Sen. Markwayne Mullin's nomination with two Democrats joining all Republicans, while Rand Paul, Senate Homeland Security Committee Chairman, withheld support.

Amid the shutdown, airports have seen operational chaos as TSA agents remain unpaid, the president said he would send ICE agents to assist TSA, and a final confirmation vote is expected in the coming days.

Sen. Markwayne Mullin, a former MMA fighter and businessman, has served in the U.S. Senate since 2023 and says his leadership will focus on 'empowering people', earning praise from Republicans and some Democrats.
title: Senate advances Markwayne Mullin's nomination for DHS Secretary in procedural vote

bias: Left outlets emphasize government aggression, using phrases like "swiftly remove" to portray urgency and human-impact concerns, while right outlets highlight enforcement and legality with language such as "pushes forward," "dissolve the injunction," and "blocking," even employing dismissive labels like "Maryland Man" and partisan mockery .
Centers frame the move as procedural—"ready to deport.

Pivotal divides include whether the injunction is a protective safeguard or a mere impediment and the legal rationale for Liberia ; all perspectives nonetheless agree the administration seeks removal and a court order currently prevents it.
summary: The Department of Justice filed Friday urging U.S. District Judge Paula Xinis to dissolve the injunction blocking Kilmar Abrego Garcia 's deportation to Liberia.

He was mistakenly deported last March to El Salvador's CECOT mega-prison despite a 2019 immigration judge order, then returned to the U.S. in June to face human-smuggling charges in Nashville, Tenn. federal courthouse.
The removal contractor told the court that a charter plane could send him to Liberia within five days, and ICE officials said removal would be imminent if the injunction is lifted.

Last month, U.S. District Judge Paula Xinis converted an emergency order into longer-term injunctive relief, criticized DOJ attorneys for misrepresenting Liberia as the only option, while they argue a permanent injunction is 'a clear error of law.'
The administration claims Kilmar Abrego Garcia is an MS‑13 member, which he and his attorneys deny, and he has pleaded not guilty while the Supreme Court ordered his return.
title: Trump administration ready to deport Kilmar Abrego Garcia to Liberia, asks judge to lift block

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes political causation and urgency—using phrases like "Trump’s war with Iran," "eat up," and "spiking/soaring gas prices" to link the conflict to broader economic harms and warn tax refunds will be consumed—while right-leaning pieces frame prices more as economically "linked to" the Iran conflict and foreground numerical offsets (Stanford’s ~$740 extra fuel cost vs.
Tax Foundation’s ~$748 refund) to argue the windfall may be neutralized; center pieces "could erase" or "threaten to erode" and stress market data .
Both sides agree prices rose and refunds may be offset, reflecting political versus policy priorities.
summary: On March 19, economists warned higher oil prices after the Iran war could offset the $748 average tax refund rise under the One Big, Beautiful Bill Act for U.S. households.
As the war closed the Strait of Hormuz, halting 20% of global oil flows, Brent crude futures neared $112 and WTI crude futures hit $99.52, pushing the national average gas price to $3.88.

Data from the Bank of America Institute show gas spending rose 14.4% in the week ended March 14, while Stanford's model estimates households will pay $740 more this year, nearly offsetting tax refunds.

Lower- and middle-income households face heavier burdens as they spend nearly 4% of earnings on gas while receiving smaller refunds, and economists expect higher prices to worsen inflation and slow growth this year.

Oxford Economics calculates that if gas averages $3.70 this year, consumers would spend about $70 billion, exceeding the $60 billion in increased refunds, while economists warn the rocket and feathers pricing dynamic could keep pump costs high.
title: Rising Gas Prices May Offset Larger 2026 Tax Refunds for U.S. Households, Analysis Finds

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the trend as rising "anti-science" sentiment, casting parents who "refuse" newborn care as morally troubling—invoking "innocent and vulnerable" infants and doctors "alarmed"—while right-leaning coverage emphasizes parental autonomy and " medical mistrust," portraying refusals as deliberate skepticism and downplaying institutional prescriptions.
Pivotal divides include causal claims and appeals to pediatric guidance .
Both cite a JAMA study and anecdotes—about half of babies on one Idaho day and over a quarter on another—agreeing vitamin K prevents potentially deadly bleeding.
summary: A recent study found that refusals of vitamin K shots for newborns nearly doubled from 2.9% to 5.2% between 2017 and 2024, indicating rising medical mistrust beyond vaccines .
Doctors are alarmed that skepticism fueled by rising anti-science sentiment is reaching proven, routine preventive care for babies, like vitamin K shots to prevent dangerous bleeding.

Research shows that newborns who don't get a vitamin K shot are 81 times more likely to develop severe bleeding than those who do, and doctors in Idaho reported 8 deaths from vitamin K deficiency bleeding in 13 months.
title: Parents Are Refusing Routine Preventative Care for Newborns at Rising Rates, Study Finds

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes that the comptroller’s figures—about $368 million total and roughly $81,700 per unsheltered person—signal that more spending "isn’t helping," framing the numbers as evidence of ineffective policy and poor taxpayer value; right-leaning coverage sensationalizes the same figures with words like "soared," "eye-popping," and comparisons to "median income," portraying them as waste, mismanagement, and political culpability, sometimes invoking "robbed us blind.

The center focuses on accountability—"can’t track the money"—and methodological limits; all sides agree high per-person costs and questions about effectiveness/accountability demand scrutiny, reflecting broader fiscal-versus-welfare divides.
summary: A New York State Comptroller report found last year that New York City spent roughly $81,228 per unsheltered homeless individual in FY 2025, totaling $368 million.
Rising systemwide costs explain part of the increase, as the unsheltered population rose from 3,588 in FY 2019 to 4,504 in FY 2025, reflecting spending on shelters, medical care, and administration.

State auditors said they cannot assess effectiveness because the Department of Homeless Services does not report expenses clearly, while prior probes flagged governance issues at New York City nonprofit shelter providers.

Given the New York State Comptroller's Office findings, the report urges adding basic metrics to the Mayor's Management Report to address efficiency and accountability questions amid increased homelessness spending by Mayor's Office.
City projections show per‑person costs rising to ~$97K in FY 2026, and exclusions such as supportive housing and unsheltered individuals complicate comparisons.
title: NYC spends more per homeless person than a typical household earns in a year, data shows

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames planning as an imminent "invasion," spotlighting detainee/"Prisoners" logistics and JSOC/elite unit deployments to stress humanitarian and moral costs, while right-leaning pieces use alarmist tactics—all‑caps "BREAKING REPORT," "full-scale preparations," and emphasis on market fallout—to portray decisive readiness and to push back at outlets that "said he's not.
Center coverage hedges with phrases like "heavy preparations" or "mulling," de-emphasizing lurid terms.

Pivotal divides are treatment of detainee planning, market impact, and Trump’s denials—yet all sides cite the same unnamed reporting and note authorization remains unclear, reflecting deeper hawk‑versus‑restraint faultlines.
summary: The Pentagon has drafted plans for potential U.S. ground deployment in Iran, according to a report.
President Trump said he is not putting troops anywhere currently, but added: "If I were, I certainly wouldn't tell you."
The U.S. military buildup has continued with thousands of Marines being sent to the Middle East.
title: Pentagon Drafts Plans For Possible US Ground Deployment In Iran: Report

bias: Left outlets frame Reddit’s biometric push as urgent, describing an AI "flood" and amplifying the CEO’s "most lightweight way" pitch as a technical fix; right outlets present it as an enforcement-minded security step—"pledging" to "protect authentic user engagement" and defend platform tradition—while center coverage highlights privacy and user-acceptance doubts, citing "just don't know how to...".

Pivotal dividing lines are anonymity versus verification, privacy/regulatory risk, and how severe the bot problem is; all sides nonetheless report biometric trials, the anti-bot goal, and leadership debate, reflecting tech-solution, order/security, and civil-liberty priorities.
summary: Reddit CEO Steve Huffman revealed the platform may soon require some users to verify their identities using Face ID, Touch ID, or passkey technology to combat bots and AI-generated content flooding the site.
A 2025 study found about 15% of Reddit posts were AI-generated, contributing to concerns over authenticity and user trust.
Researchers exploited Reddit's r/changemyview subreddit in 2025 by deploying AI bots posing as diverse personas, influencing discussions with 1,700 comments before being banned.
Reddit users have mixed reactions to biometric verification, with some fearing loss of anonymity and privacy while others support efforts to reduce fake and botted content.
title: Reddit CEO Says Platform is Exploring Face ID to Fight Bots

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Democrats as escalating attacks on a rising GOP figure—using terms like "heir apparent to the MAGA coalition" and "sharpened criticism" to signal threat and strategic repositioning away from Trump—while right-leaning pieces portray Democrats as politically opportunistic, foregrounding Beshear’s charge that Vance "had abandoned the communities" and invoking his "memoir" to imply hypocrisy.
Rhetorically the left leans cautionary/threat-oriented, the right leans accusatory/moralizing; centrists de-emphasize named partisan actors and stick to procedural framing.
Both sides agree Democrats are zeroing in on Vance even as Trump remains a primary target, revealing broader cultural and partisan divides.
summary: Democrats say they should begin defining JD Vance now rather than waiting, as he is a clear front-runner for the 2028 nomination.
Last year, Democrats began targeting Vice President JD Vance, whose memoir Hillbilly Elegy explains his working-class roots central to his appeal.

On Saturday, Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear traveled to Vance's home county and told a Butler County fundraiser Vance had abandoned those communities, calling Hillbilly Elegy 'trafficked in tired stereotypes' and 'hillbilly hate'.
Some Democrats warn vice presidents can be vulnerable, as party strategists note Vance’s working-class image is under attack, leaving him exposed ahead of 2028.
Vance's youth — he is 41 — shapes his profile, and Beshear says Democrats can win back voters in Trump country by focusing on affordable health care and public safety.
title: Democrats sharpen criticism of Vance as they look past Trump to the 2028 presidential campaign

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Iran ’s warning as a humanitarian alarm—using phrases like "lives and livelihoods at risk" and stressing "completely close" plus claims that only ~5% of pre‑war shipping remains—portraying Trump’s 48‑hour ultimatum as provocative.

Right-Leaning pieces emphasize security and reciprocity, labeling Tehran a "regime", calling the exchange "tit‑for‑tat", spotlighting threats to US companies and even quoting forceful language like "obliterate" to justify a hawkish stance and warn of an indefinite blockade.
Center reporting de‑emphasizes partisan moralizing, foregrounding IEA market risks and regional escalation; both sides nonetheless agree threats imperil the Strait and energy flows.
summary: On Sunday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps announced it would 'completely close' the Strait of Hormuz if the United States targets Iranian energy infrastructure, responding to President Donald Trump 's 48-hour ultimatum set late on Saturday.

Retaliating after earlier strikes, Iran said its attacks in the Negev Desert hit the Natanz nuclear enrichment site and a hospital in Andimeshk, causing evacuations and scores wounded in Arad and Dimona.
Roughly one-fifth of global oil supply passes through the strait, and tanker traffic has nearly halted, with casualty totals exceeding 2,000 and Iran's death toll surpassing 1,000.

The Pentagon declined to comment while US officials say Iran's Revolutionary Guard controls much of Iran's infrastructure used in the war, and Tehran claims it permits safe passage for non-enemy vessels despite practically closing the strait.
The International Atomic Energy Agency reported most of Iran's estimated 440kg of enriched uranium is beneath rubble at Isfahan, while Israel aims to weaken Iran's nuclear and missile programs.
title: Iran threatens to 'completely' close Strait of Hormuz and hit power plants after Trump ultimatum

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as humanitarian harm—using charged words like "wrecks" and "cluster munition" and highlighting damage to a kindergarten and religious sites—while right-leaning outlets frame it as a security assault, accusing Iran of intent with phrases like "targets civilians," stressing visuals/"shocking images" and concrete tolls and saying a "major disaster was averted.
Center reporting de-emphasized cultural damage and emphasized technical issues—"splitting warhead," "Military Says"—and interceptor gaps.

Key divides are intent attribution, defense failure, casualty counts and the IRGC claim; all agree debris struck an empty kindergarten, no children were hurt, and officials warned of a near-catastrophe.
summary: On Saturday morning, an Iranian cluster-style missile struck a kindergarten in Rishon Lezion, causing heavy damage with no immediate reports of casualties as air-raid alerts sounded across central Israel .
Authorities said the barrage included several projectiles launched from Iran, including a cluster munition, and Hezbollah in Lebanon continued firing missiles at Israel.

Rishon Lezion city officials reported eight impact sites, while police bomb disposal units and Rescue and Home Front Command teams responded; a man in his 40s was lightly injured, according to Shamir Medical Center.
Education Minister Yoav Kish ordered nationwide in-person class cancellations for Sunday and Monday, shifting all students to remote learning with no exceptions.

Authorities will reassess conditions ahead of Tuesday as heightened security remains active, with shelter orders covering the Tel Aviv metropolitan area, Ashdod, the Shphela lowlands and parts of the West Bank while the military urged the public to avoid impact sites.
title: Rishon Lezion kindergarten takes heavy damage from Iranian cluster munition

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Israel ’s effort as a sweeping advocacy push—using verbs like "seeking" or "pushing" and phrases such as "significant change," "tackles" and "convince" to foreground pressure for reform—while right-leaning pieces emphasize a national-security angle, reporting an ambassadorial attribution linking threats to Iran and noting calls to "limit certain 'freedoms'.
Center outlets attribute claims with quotation marks and are more restrained.

The pivotal divides are the alleged freedom restrictions and Iran link; all agree Israel says Canada "must do more" and portray a diplomatic/public-relations campaign, yet Canadian response and outside expert or civil-liberty views are de-emphasized.
summary: From Israel's presidency to its envoy in Ottawa, officials are urging Canada to do more to curb threats against Jews, as reported by The Canadian Press on March 17, 2026.

After a spate of local attacks, Canadian police and Jewish organizations report rising anti-Jewish hate, including firebombings, prompting police delegations from Israel to share counterterrorism experience.

At a virtual forum last week, Israeli Ambassador Iddo Moed said 'We have a very clear objective this year, and that is to create a significant change in the way antisemitism is being dealt with here in Canada' and warned 'Some people are out there to abuse our democracy and our values so that they can dominate.'
Ottawa announced $10 million to boost security at synagogues and community centres as Bill C-9 heads tentatively to third reading in the House of Commons next week.

Mira Sucharov said Israel's outreach serves both security protection and broader foreign-policy aims as Israeli Ambassador Iddo Moed suggested Canada may need to constrain certain freedoms without specifying which, while Israeli President Isaac Herzog convened a March 9 call with Toronto-area Jewish leaders.
title: Israel seeking ‘significant change’ in how Canada tackles antisemitism

bias: Right-Leaning coverage seizes the moment as political theater—using words like "mocks," "blunder," "discombobulated," "viral," and "hilarious" to cast Schumer’s "We must fund ICE" slip as humiliating hypocrisy and to assert he’s been "blocking" ICE funding—while center coverage frames it as a "gaffe" situated in the DHS funding impasse and national-security/traveler-impact arguments; left-leaning perspective is de-emphasized in these analyses.

Pivotal dividing lines are claims of hypocrisy versus policy context and ridicule versus strategic messaging; both sides nonetheless agree Schumer said the line and Republicans harnessed it, reflecting deeper cultural and partisan priorities.
summary: Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer mistakenly said, 'We must fund ICE,' but quickly corrected to 'We must fund TSA now' during a Senate debate on federal funding amid a government shutdown.

Democrats have blocked full funding for the Department of Homeland Security, including the TSA, to push for restrictions on immigration enforcement, contributing to operational issues for TSA, Secret Service, and Coast Guard.
The funding lapse began on February 13, causing TSA staff shortages and long airport wait times during a busy spring break travel season with an expected 171 million travelers.
Republican leaders, including Senate Majority Leader John Thune, urged Democrats to support full DHS funding to resolve the shutdown, citing national security concerns amid threats from Iran.
title: Trump comments on Schumer’s ‘fund ICE’ remark during Senate remarks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage portrays the confrontation as a reckoning for "probe failures" in the Epstein case, using the "brutal" label "pedophile protector" to highlight conservative complicity.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets frame the event as Bongino "erupting" against accusations of being a "coward" regarding the " Deep State .
While the left emphasizes a single "ex-cop" challenger, the right identifies two specific activists, signaling internal ideological purges.
Both sides present Bongino as a "Former FBI Deputy Director," showcasing a shared reliance on the same designation.
Ultimately, both perspectives center on the Epstein investigation, reflecting a broader, bipartisan distrust of institutional power.
summary: Dan Bongino, former FBI Deputy Director, was publicly confronted in Florida by Joseph Phillip Daniel regarding Bongino's handling of the Jeffrey Epstein investigation.
Joseph Phillip Daniel, a conservative Christian preacher, accused Dan Bongino of being a 'pedophile protector' during the confrontation.
The confrontation escalated with Daniel shouting criticisms and Bongino responding defiantly, telling Daniel to 'go volunteer' and accusing him of inaction.
Security intervened and escorted Dan Bongino away to end the heated public confrontation.
title: Man confronts Dan Bongino over handling of Jeffrey Epstein investigation

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the DOJ probe as politically costly and personalizes blame—using charged words like "botched," "backfire spectacularly," and "shadow Fed chair," naming Pirro and portraying incompetence; center outlets use cautious phrasing like "could backfire" and de-emphasize colorful characterizations.
Right-Leaning angles are de-emphasized here.
Pivotal divides include naming a prosecutor and labeling the case "botched," which yield starkly different portrayals of DOJ competence versus political risk.

Both perspectives agree Powell is Fed chair, Trump sought his removal, and reporting suggests the probe could extend Powell's tenure, reflecting broader ideological priorities about accountability versus power.
summary: President Donald Trump has pressed for Jerome Powell's removal for months as the Justice Department launched a criminal probe into Powell's congressional testimony, led by D.C. U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro.
A federal judge this month invalidated two subpoenas the Justice Department issued to the Fed, effectively dismantling the criminal probe, and Pirro vowed to appeal.
Powell made clear this past week he will not resign from the Fed's Board of Governors until the Justice Department investigation is resolved, saying, 'That is what the law calls for.'
A key Republican senator has committed to blocking Kevin Warsh's confirmation until the Powell investigation concludes, complicating the Senate confirmation process.
With Powell's term ending in less than two months and Warsh stalled, the investigation risks creating a shadow Fed chair, observers warn.
title: Powell Probe Could Backfire on DOJ

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the influx of "Big money" and "partisan groups" as corrosive, using charged verbs like "revving up" and highlighting corporate actors; right-leaning pieces treat funding more as descriptive battleground language—"legal battles"—and emphasize careerism with "springboard to higher office," while de-emphasizing donor detail.
Center outlets supply figures that the others often omit, a dividing line that fuels leftward alarm and rightward restraint.
Both sides nonetheless agree these AG races are nationalized and consequential, a split tied to broader ideological and institutional fault lines.
summary: Last year Republican and Democratic party groups raised record sums to prepare for 2026 attorney general races, with Republican organizations reporting $29 million and Democratic counterparts bringing in $28 million, nearly twice their usual fundraising at this election stage.

Adam Piper, executive director of the Republican Attorneys General Association, said donors are drawn to the office's political potential as a springboard to higher office. Former Maine attorney general James Tierney noted states increasingly use lawsuits to solve problems, elevating the position's national importance.

Major donors include technology and tobacco companies, law firms, unions and ideological groups funding priority contests across roughly 30 seats this year, with parties targeting swing states including Texas, Minnesota and New York.

Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes said her office joined 38 lawsuits and saved the state $1.5 billion, while Republican candidate Rodney Glassman campaigned to halt such prosecutions if he defeats Senate President Warren Peterson in the June 22 primary.

In Texas a May 26 runoff will pit U.S. Rep. Chip Roy against state Sen. Mayes Middleton for the party nomination, while at least six current attorneys general are running for governor this year, underscoring the office's career trajectory.
title: Record Corporate Donations Surge in 2026 State Attorney General Races

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes a celebratory cultural narrative—repeating words like "tribute" and highlighting the statues as a festive nod to the 250th and their "larger than real-life" scale—using a warm, ceremonious tone.

Right-Leaning pieces foreground patriotic symbolism and formal legitimacy—phrases like "America's," "official," and "public debut"—and stress the law that named the bison, framing the installation as an institutional honor.
Pivotal divides include the legal designation and the absence of Indigenous or funding context .
Center reports are succinctly descriptive; all agree on three bronze statues, location, and the anniversary linkage.
summary: The national mammal of the United States , the bison, has three bronze statues on permanent display outside the Smithsonian's National Museum of Natural History in Washington to celebrate America's 250th birthday.
Bison were nearly driven to extinction in the 1800s, but conservation efforts have revived their population, as the statues symbolize.
Paleoartist Gary Staab sculpted the larger-than-life statues, depicting a bull, cow, and calf, which took four months to complete.
title: America’s official mammal, the bison, gets a bronze tribute for the country’s 250th birthday

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Houthis through political and ideological lenses—highlighting mass demonstrations, terms like "battle-hardened militia" and "last strategic card," and stressing the risk of regional escalation—while right-leaning pieces emphasize security and economic threat, using labels like "terrorist group," phrases such as "threaten," "block," and incendiary language like "mullahs choke Strait of Hormuz" to portray deliberate Iranian proxy strategy.
Center outlets often foreground explicit quotes—e.g., "We will strike American ships"—and sober threat assessments.
Pivotal divides include the "terrorist" label, emphasis on economic choke points versus political motives, and humanitarian or diplomatic responses; all sides agree Iran links and shipping risks.
summary: On Friday, reports said Yemen 's Ansar Allah consider closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait while limiting themselves to threats since the war broke out last month.
Drawing on past tactics, the Houthis consider blocking the Bab al-Mandab to support Iran and pressure the West, citing attacks on shipping linked to Israel and the US since October 2023.

Analysts note the group's arsenal and posture: the Houthis have sophisticated missiles and drones able to strike hundreds of kilometres, while Abdul Malik al-Houthi said, 'Our fingers are on the trigger, ready to respond at any moment should developments warrant it'.

If the Bab al-Mandeb Strait is blocked, global shipping firms and tankers would reroute around Africa, raising shipping costs, fuel prices, and inflation worldwide, as the route supplies up to seven million barrels of oil daily.

Strategically, the Houthis face political and financial trade-offs, as firing at the Red Sea could end their independence from Tehran and provoke retaliation from Saudi Arabia, Gulf allies, the US, and Israel.
title: Houthis Could Enter Iran War As Soon As Monday

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes systemic disruption, framing the event as a "major" threat that "forces" closures to highlight public harm.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets prioritize institutional resilience, portraying staff as heroic for performing a "life-saving transplant" despite the "system outage.
While the left focuses on the closure of 35 clinics, the right de-emphasizes these losses for human-interest narratives.
Center-Leaning reports provide unique financial context, noting revenue "tumbled" by 20%.

Both perspectives acknowledge staff used "pen and paper" for nine days, reflecting shared concerns over healthcare vulnerability within a broader landscape of digital extortion and regional instability.
summary: In February, the University of Mississippi Medical Center reported revenue fell about 20% below budget, missing roughly $34.2 million of an expected $194.1 million.

Security reporting shows the suspected Russia-linked Medusa ransomware-as-a-service claimed responsibility and threatened to expose UMMC data unless an $800,000 ransom was paid, while UMMC said the incident remains under investigation.
Clinic operations were disrupted when statewide clinics affiliated with UMMC closed and staff used paper charts for nine days, delaying approximately 650 surgeries until March 2.

To address the backlog, UMMC has extended clinic and operating room hours, and Sinclair says net income is about $65 million behind budget for the year, with all charges expected to be processed by the end of March.
During the Feb. 18 transplant, clinicians completed Wade Watts' surgery amid the outage, with his family praising their care despite electronic system failures.
title: UMMC Revenue Fell 20% After Ransomware Attack Shut Clinics for Nine Days

bias: Right-Leaning coverage foregrounds an individualized, sensational narrative—naming the homeowner, citing a "football-sized" rock and using charged verbs like "smashed" and phrases such as "gaping hole" and "panic"—while center outlets emphasize cautious, evidence-focused framing, hedging with "possible"/"suspected meteorite," citing NASA , and stressing wider geographic sightings, timing, and two‑story structural damage.

Pivotal dividing lines are homeowner ID and rock size, the "green flash" observation, and precise timestamps, which provoke starkly different tones; both sides nonetheless note a loud boom, a visible "fireball," and widespread social‑media reports.
summary: On Saturday, March 21, 2026, NASA confirmed a meteor broke apart over Southeast Texas after moving southeast at 35,000 mph and fragmenting about 49 miles above Stagecoach.
Witnesses said at about 4:40 p.m. CDT, a fireball appeared and the boom lasted between 1.5 and 7.5 seconds, despite clear skies.
Local newsrooms received reports pouring in from Katy, Cypress and Pearland, while the American Meteor Society logged more than 100 reports of the fireball event.

Ponderosa Fire Chief Fred Windisch and responding crews, including Fire Captain Tyler Ellingham, said they found an 'unusual rock' that may be debris from a possible meteorite that crashed through a home’s roof.

The National Weather Service said a satellite product detected a possible meteor as local crews checked explosions, while NASA said fragmentation of an object weighing about a ton with a diameter of 3 feet caused pressure waves creating loud booms.
title: Suspected Meteorite Crashes Through Houston Home After Fireball Lights Up Texas Sky
